Directions: Read the following passage carefully and answer the items that follow the passage. Your answers to these items should be based solely on the passage.

Can agricultural productivity meet human needs in the long run? Since twentieth century advances, given increasing costs, do not seem sustainable, further increases in output will not be achieved easily. Global population growth, while showing signs of slowing down, has pushed farmers and herders into areas unsuited to intensive agriculture, accelerating worldwide ecological disruptions. Reserves of arable land and fresh water have diminished alarmingly. Chemical runoffs have compounded pollution problems. Invasion of natural habitats have hastened species extinction. Desertification has been severe in the last hundred years, especially in North Africa, southern portions of Africa, Australia, northern Mexico, and the American Southwest. Tropical forests have shrunk by one-half since World War II, with agricultural pressures causing three-quarters of that loss. Government inter- vention has produced its share of catastrophes, ranging from a terrible famine in China between 1958-1961 that may have killed 30 million people and destroyed the Aral Sea. Taking everything into consideration, we may remain hopeful about humankind's agricultural prospects, but we can scarcely look forward optimistically at the end of the first quarter of the twenty-first century.
en.content: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->Why do twentieth century advances not seem sustainable?

options.content: They do not meet the human needs
Due to the increased cost of agricultural production
Because they are outdated for the current times

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 2: Due to the increased cost of agricultural production.

Step-by-step:

  • The passage says: “Since twentieth century advances, given increasing costs, do not seem sustainable…”

  • This clearly tells us that these advances are not sustainable because of increasing costs.

  • So the reason is the increased cost of agricultural production.

Therefore, the answer is:

$$\text{Option 2}$$

Directions: The following items have a sentence in direct or indirect speech, followed by four options. One of the options correctly converts the direct speech into indirect speech. Select the most appropriate option and mark your answer on the Answer Sheet.

Convert from direct into indirect speech.

Ayushi said to the group, "We have had enough of suffering today. It is time to march forward."

options.content: Ayushi told the group that they had had enough of suffering that day and it was time to march forward
Ayushi asked the group that they had had enough of suffering this day and it was time to march forward
Ayushi told the group that they had enough of suffering and that it is time to march forward
Ayushi told the group that we had had enough of suffering this day and it was time to march forward

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 1.

$$ \text{Ayushi told the group that they had had enough of suffering that day and it was time to march forward.} $$

Step-by-step explanation:

The direct speech is:

$$ \text{Ayushi said to the group, "We have had enough of suffering today. It is time to march forward."} $$

Rules used:

  1. "said to" changes to "told" when an object is given.

    $$ \text{said to the group} \rightarrow \text{told the group} $$

  2. Pronoun changes according to the reporting speech.

    $$ \text{We} \rightarrow \text{they} $$

    because Ayushi is speaking to the group.

  3. Present perfect changes to past perfect in indirect speech.

    $$ \text{have had} \rightarrow \text{had had} $$

  4. "today" changes to "that day".

    $$ \text{today} \rightarrow \text{that day} $$

  5. The second sentence also becomes part of the reported speech:

    $$ \text{It is time} \rightarrow \text{it was time} $$

Why the other options are wrong:

  • Option 2: uses asked, which is incorrect because this is not a question.

  • Option 3: does not correctly change the tense and keeps is instead of was.

  • Option 4: keeps we and this day, which are incorrect in indirect speech.

So, the most appropriate option is Option 1.

Directions: The following items have a sentence in direct or indirect speech, followed by four options. One of the options correctly converts the direct speech into indirect speech. Select the most appropriate option and mark your answer on the Answer Sheet.

Convert from direct speech into indirect speech.

"Where were you last week?" Alok asked Sheela

options.content: Alok told Sheela where she had been the week before
Alok asked Sheela where she had been the week before
Alok asked Sheela where she was the week before
Alok asked Sheela where she had been the last week

en.explanation:
The correct answer is:

Option 2: Alok asked Sheela where she had been the week before.

Step-by-step:

The direct speech is:

$$ \text{"Where were you last week?" Alok asked Sheela.} $$

Rules used:

  1. Since it is a question, asked is the correct reporting verb.

  2. The question word where remains the same.

  3. In indirect speech, you changes to she because Alok is asking Sheela.

  4. The past tense were changes to had been.

  5. last week changes to the week before.

So the correct indirect speech is:

$$ \text{Alok asked Sheela where she had been the week before.} $$

Why others are incorrect:

  • Option 1: Uses told, but this is a question, so asked should be used.

  • Option 3: was should change to had been.

  • Option 4: last week should change to the week before.

Answer: Option 2

Directions : Following items have sentences in active voice with four options. One of the options converts the active voice into passive voice correctly. Select the most appropriate option and mark your answer on the Answer Sheet. 

The king who the people loved built the castle five centuries ago

options.content: The castle was built five centuries ago by the king who was loved by the people
The castle was built five centuries ago by the king who loved the people
The castle built five centuries ago by the king loved by the people
The castle was built five centuries ago by the people who were loved by the king

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 1.

Step-by-step:

Active sentence:

$$\text{The king who the people loved built the castle five centuries ago.}$$

  1. The object in the active voice is:

$$\text{the castle}$$

  1. In passive voice, the object becomes the subject:

$$\text{The castle}$$

  1. The verb is:

$$\text{built}$$

This is in the simple past tense, so in passive voice we use:

$$\text{was built}$$

  1. The doer is:

$$\text{the king who the people loved}$$

This becomes:

$$\text{by the king who was loved by the people}$$

So the correct passive form is:

$$\text{The castle was built five centuries ago by the king who was loved by the people.}$$

Why others are wrong:

  • Option 2 changes the meaning.

    It says the king loved the people, but the original says the people loved the king.

  • Option 3 is grammatically incomplete because was is missing.

  • Option 4 changes the subject completely and gives the wrong meaning.

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 1}}$$

Directions : Following items have sentences in active voice with four options. One of the options converts the active voice into passive voice correctly. Select the most appropriate option and mark your answer on the Answer Sheet. 

The company has recruited four hundred engineers who will have to complete an internship for three weeks

options.content: Four hundred engineers have been recruited by the company and an internship of three weeks will have to be completed by them
Four hundred engineers have been recruited by the company and they will have to complete an internship of three weeks
The company has been recruited by four hundred engineers and an internship of three week will have to be completed by them
Four hundred engineers have been recruited by the company. Internship of three weeks will have had to be completed by them

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 1.

Step-by-step:

The active sentence is:

The company has recruited four hundred engineers who will have to complete an internship for three weeks.

To change into passive voice:

  1. The object “four hundred engineers” becomes the subject.

  2. “has recruited” becomes “have been recruited”.

  3. “by the company” is added.

  4. The clause “who will have to complete an internship for three weeks” changes into passive as

    “an internship of three weeks will have to be completed by them.”

So the correct passive form is:

Four hundred engineers have been recruited by the company and an internship of three weeks will have to be completed by them.

Why others are wrong:

  • Option 2: Second part is still in active voice.

  • Option 3: Meaning becomes incorrect; the company cannot be recruited by engineers.

  • Option 4: “will have had to be completed” is grammatically incorrect here.

Final Answer:

Option 1

Directions :In the following items, a pair of words is provided. You are required to select the option that most appropriately describes the meaning of both the words and mark your response on the Answer Sheet accordingly 

Overview and Review

options.content: Overview means assessment and review means general idea
Overview means generai idea and review means assessment
Overview means general analysis and review means go through
Overview means summary and review means evaluation

en.explanation:
To find the correct option, let us understand the exact meanings of both words step by step.

Step 1: Understand the meaning of "Overview"

The word 'Overview' refers to a broad or general view of a subject. It provides the main points without going into deep details. For example, before starting a new chapter, a teacher might give an overview of the topic. Therefore, an overview simply gives a general idea of something.

Step 2: Understand the meaning of "Review"

The word 'Review' means to examine or study something carefully, usually to make a judgment, find mistakes, or suggest improvements. For example, a teacher might review your test paper to check your performance. Therefore, a review involves a formal and detailed assessment.

Step 3: Analyze the given options

  • Option 1: States that overview is an assessment and review is a general idea. This is incorrect because it completely reverses the meanings of the two words.

  • Option 2: States that overview means a general idea and review means assessment. This perfectly matches the distinct definitions we discussed.

  • Option 3: Uses the terms "general analysis" and "go through". An overview is not an analysis (which requires detailed study), making this incorrect.

  • Option 4: Uses "summary" and "evaluation". While close, Option 2 is the most precise and standard definition used in English grammar to contrast the broadness of an overview (general idea) with the critical examination of a review (assessment).

Therefore, the option that most appropriately describes both words is Option 2.

Correct Answer:

Option 2: Overview means general idea and review means assessment

Directions : In this section each item consists of six sentences of a passage. The first and sixth sentences are given in the beginning as S1 and S6. The middle four sentences in each have been jumbled up and labelled as P, Q, R and S. You are required to find the proper sequence of the four sentences and mark your response accordingly on the Answer Sheet. 

S1: Sufism is an English word coined in the Nineteenth Century 

S6 : It may also have been suffa, the platform outside a place of Islamic worship

P. Historians have understood this term in several ways

Q. According to some scholars, it is derived from suf, meaning wool 

R. Others derive it from safa, meaning purity 

S. The word used for Sufism in Islamic texts is tasawwuf
en.explanation:
The correct sequence is:

$$\boxed{\text{SPQR}}$$

Step-by-step:

  • $S1$ says: Sufism is an English word coined in the Nineteenth Century.

  • After this, sentence $S$ should come because it gives the corresponding Islamic term:

  • The word used for Sufism in Islamic texts is tasawwuf.

  • Then sentence $P$ fits:

  • Historians have understood this term in several ways.

  • Here, "this term" refers to tasawwuf.

  • After that, the different explanations come in order:

  • $Q$: According to some scholars, it is derived from suf, meaning wool

  • $R$: Others derive it from safa, meaning purity

  • Finally, $S6$ adds one more possible origin:

  • It may also have been suffa…

So the full order is:

$$S1 - S - P - Q - R - S6$$

Correct option:

$$\boxed{2}$$

en.content:
Directions : In this section each item consists of six sentences of a passage. The first and sixth sentences are given in the beginning as S1 and S6. The middle four sentences in each have been jumbled up and labelled as P, Q, R and S. You are required to find the proper sequence of the four sentences and mark your response accordingly on the Answer Sheet. 

S1: Al-Biruni was born in 973, in Khwarizm in _ present-day Uzbekistan 

S6 : He arrived in Ghazni as a hostage, but gradually developed a liking for the city

P. In 1017, when Sultan Mahmud invaded Khwarizm, he _ took several scholars back to his capital, Ghazni : Al-Biruni was one among them

Q. He was well versed in several languages; Syriac, Arabic, Persian, Hebrew, and Sanskrit  

R. Khwarizm was an important centre of learning, and Al-Biruni received the best education available at the time  

S. However he did not know Greek, although he was familiar with the works of Plato and other Greek philosophers
en.explanation:
To find the correct sequence of the jumbled sentences, let us analyze the logical flow of the passage step-by-step:

  1. Connecting to S1:

Sentence S1 states that Al-Biruni was born in Khwarizm. Sentence R directly continues this topic by describing Khwarizm as a centre of learning where Al-Biruni received his education. This makes R the perfect continuation of S1.

  1. Forming a pair (Q and S):

Sentence Q mentions that Al-Biruni was well versed in several languages. Sentence S starts with the contrasting word "However" and states that he did not know Greek. Because S introduces an exception to the languages he knew in Q, Q and S form a mandatory pair (QS).

  1. Connecting to S6:

Sentence P tells us about the historical event in 1017 when Sultan Mahmud took scholars, including Al-Biruni, to Ghazni. Sentence S6 picks up exactly from here, stating how he arrived in Ghazni as a hostage and slowly developed a liking for the city. Therefore, P must be the sentence immediately preceding S6.

By putting these clues together, the logical sequence is R - Q - S - P.

Correct Sequence:

S1: Al-Biruni was born in 973, in Khwarizm in present-day Uzbekistan

R: Khwarizm was an important centre of learning, and Al-Biruni received the best education available at the time

Q: He was well versed in several languages; Syriac, Arabic, Persian, Hebrew, and Sanskrit

S: However he did not know Greek, although he was familiar with the works of Plato and other Greek philosophers

P: In 1017, when Sultan Mahmud invaded Khwarizm, he took several scholars back to his capital, Ghazni : Al-Biruni was one among them

S6: He arrived in Ghazni as a hostage, but gradually developed a liking for the city

The proper sequence is RQSP.

Correct Answer:

Option 2

Directions : In this section each item consists of six sentences of a passage. The first and sixth sentences are given in the beginning as S1 and S6. The middle four sentences in each have been jumbled up and labelled as P, Q, R and S. You are required to find the proper sequence of the four sentences and mark your response accordingly on the Answer Sheet. 

S1: Vijayanagara or ‘city of victory’ was the name of both a city and an empire 

S6 : They remembered it as Hampi, a name derived from that of the local mother goddess, Pampadevi

P. In its heyday it stretched from the river Krishna in the north to the extreme south of the Indian peninsula

Q. Although it fell into ruin in the seventeenth-eighteenth centuries, it continued to live in the memories of the people who inhabited the Krishna-Tungabhadra doab 

R. In 1565 the city was sacked and subsequently deserted

S. The Empire was founded in the fourteenth century
en.explanation:
The correct sequence is:

$$\boxed{\text{SPRQ}}$$

Step-by-step:

  • $S1$ introduces Vijayanagara as both a city and an empire.

  • $S$ should come first: it tells us when the empire was founded.

  • $P$ follows naturally: after introducing the empire, it describes its extent.

  • $R$ then tells what happened later: in $1565$, the city was sacked and deserted.

  • $Q$ comes after that because it begins with “Although it fell into ruin…”, which refers to the destruction mentioned in $R$.

  • Finally, $S6$ says that people remembered it as Hampi.

So the full order is:

$$S1 \rightarrow S \rightarrow P \rightarrow R \rightarrow Q \rightarrow S6$$

Hence, the answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 1}}$$

S1: Liberalisation and _ globalisation freed India’s economy from the low GDP trap that had impeded India’s progress 

S6 : Today India has transformed into one of the largest economies of the world

P. India’s 3 pe rate of growth and a set of emergent circumstances led to a balance of payment crisis in the early 1990s 

Q. Ease of business was ensured, and the requirement for import licences, and production quotas, were removed

R. Apart from these measures India also took a loan from the IMF to tide over the crisis, but after 1993, India has not taken another loan from the IMF

S. In a landmark budget in 1991, responding to the emergent crisis, India delicenced many industries and liberalised its economic policies
en.explanation:
The correct sequence is:

$$\boxed{\text{PSQR}}$$

So, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4}}$$

Step-by-step logic:

  • P should come first because it explains why reforms were needed:

  • India faced a balance of payment crisis in the early 1990s.

  • S comes next because it tells what India did in response:

  • In 1991, India delicensed industries and liberalised economic policies.

  • Q follows naturally because it gives further details of those reforms:

  • Ease of business was ensured.

  • Import licences and production quotas were removed.

  • R comes after that because “Apart from these measures” clearly refers to the reforms already mentioned in S and Q:

  • India also took a loan from the IMF.

Thus the full order is:

$$S1 \rightarrow P \rightarrow S \rightarrow Q \rightarrow R \rightarrow S6$$

Final answer:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4: PSQR}}$$

The middle four sentences in each have been jumbled up and labelled as P, Q, R and S. You are required to find the proper sequence of the four sentences and mark your response accordingly on the Answer Sheet. 

S1: In many countries, there are millions of people who are underprivileged and deprived 

S6 : In the past forced labour was imposed by landlords, moneylenders, and other wealthy persons

P. Both of these are prohibited under the Constitution of India  

Q. One such form of exploitation in our country has been begar or forced labour without payment 

R. The marginalised are often subjected to exploitation by their fellow human beings 

S. Another closely related form of exploitation is buying and selling of human beings and using them as slaves
en.explanation:
The correct sequence is:

$$\boxed{\text{RQSP}}$$

So, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 2}}$$

Step-by-step:

  • $S1$ says that many people are underprivileged and deprived.

  • The next logical sentence is $R$:

    "The marginalised are often subjected to exploitation by their fellow human beings."

    This continues the idea of deprivation and exploitation.

  • Then $Q$:

    "One such form of exploitation in our country has been begar or forced labour without payment."

    Here, "one such form" refers to exploitation mentioned in $R$.

  • Then $S$:

    "Another closely related form of exploitation is buying and selling of human beings and using them as slaves."

    The word "Another" clearly follows after one form has already been mentioned in $Q$.

  • Then $P$:

    "Both of these are prohibited under the Constitution of India."

    "Both of these" refers to the two forms mentioned in $Q$ and $S$.

  • Finally, $S6$:

    "In the past forced labour was imposed by landlords, moneylenders, and other wealthy persons."

Hence the proper order is:

$$S1 \rightarrow R \rightarrow Q \rightarrow S \rightarrow P \rightarrow S6$$

Answer: $$\boxed{\text{RQSP}}$$

Directions : Given below are sentences that use discourse markers/expressions to make them complete. Identify the most appropriate discourse marker and mark your answer accordingly on the Answer Sheet. 

I can’t be bothered to tell him what I think;______ he would not listen to me.
en.explanation:
The correct discourse marker is Option 2: in any case.

So the sentence becomes:

I can’t be bothered to tell him what I think; in any case he would not listen to me.

Why?

  • In any case means anyway or even if I did, the result would be the same.

  • Here, the speaker says there is no point in telling him because he would not listen.

Why not the others?

  • meanwhile – used for something happening at the same time.

  • in the meantime – used for the period before something else happens.

  • somehow – means in some way, but it does not fit the meaning here.

Answer: Option 2 – in any case

Complete sentence:

Explanation:

  • As regards means with reference to or regarding.

  • The sentence is talking about a particular situation involving the referee’s signal.

Why others are incorrect:

  • On the contrary is used to show the opposite of what was said.

  • Whatever more is not suitable here.

  • Briefly means in a short way, which does not fit the sentence.

So, the correct answer is:

$$\text{Option 4: As regards}$$

Directions : Given below are sentences that use discourse markers/expressions to make them complete. Identify the most appropriate discourse marker and mark your answer accordingly on the Answer Sheet. 

________  women are healthier and are living longer than ever before: indeed on an average, they can expect to live five years longer than men.
en.explanation:
The most appropriate discourse marker is:

Option 3: As far as

So the complete sentence is:

As far as women are concerned, they are healthier and are living longer than ever before; indeed, on an average, they can expect to live five years longer than men.

Why?

  • As far as is used to introduce a topic or say something about a particular group.

  • Here, the sentence is specifically talking about women, so “As far as women are concerned” fits naturally.

Why not the others?

  • To a large extent shows degree, not topic introduction.

  • As regards can also introduce a topic, but “As far as women are concerned” is the more natural and commonly used expression here.

  • Turning now is usually used when shifting to a new point in speech or writing, and it does not fit smoothly here.

Answer: Option 3

The most appropriate discourse marker is Option 3: what is more.

Complete sentence:

The difference is that I have been fortunate to find a career that I love and, what is more, I am getting paid reasonably for it.

Why?

The phrase “what is more” is used to add another positive point or give extra information.

Here, the speaker says:

  1. They love their career.

  2. In addition, they are getting paid reasonably.

So, “what is more” fits perfectly.

Why the others are incorrect?

  • briefly – means in a short way; does not fit the meaning.

  • broadly speaking – means generally; not suitable here.

  • in conclusion – used at the end of a discussion; not appropriate in this sentence.

Correct Answer:

Option 3

en.explanation:
The most appropriate discourse marker is:

Option 4: In general

So the complete sentence is:

In general, the Government of India has followed the policy of trying to improve relations with its neighbours.

Why?

  • In general is used to talk about something that is true in most cases or broadly.

  • The sentence is making a broad statement about the policy of the Government of India.

Why the others are incorrect?

  • Particular → incorrect usage here; it should be in particular if used as a discourse marker.

  • Knowingly → means deliberately, which does not fit the meaning.

  • Otherwise → shows an alternative or consequence, so it does not fit here.

Answer: Option 4 — In general

Answer: Option 1 — In that case

Complete sentence:

As such, the records have been completely undermined by shoddy book-keeping.

  • Because of needs a noun phrase after it, so it cannot stand alone here.

  • Forever does not make sense in this context.

  • Hence usually shows a result, but As such is more suitable and natural here.

Answer: Option 2 — As such

Directions : Each of the following items in this section consists of a sentence, the parts of which have been jumbled. These parts have been labelled as P, Q, R and S. of school education is an ineluctable truth (P) / that sporting activities are an integral part (Q) / meaningfully towards the overall growth of a student (R) / that underscores the learning process and contributes (S).
en.explanation:
The correct arrangement is:

$$\text{QPSR}$$

So, the correct option is:

$$\text{Option 1}$$

Correct sentence:

“That sporting activities are an integral part of school education is an ineluctable truth that underscores the learning process and contributes meaningfully towards the overall growth of a student.”

Step-by-step:

  • $Q$ starts well: “that sporting activities are an integral part”

  • $P$ completes it: “of school education is an ineluctable truth”

  • $S$ continues logically: “that underscores the learning process and contributes”

  • $R$ finishes the sentence: “meaningfully towards the overall growth of a student”

Hence, the answer is:

en.content: The rule to determine the direction of a force experienced by a straight current carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field which is perpendicular to it is
en.explanation:
The correct answer is:

Option 2: Fleming’s left-hand rule

Explanation:

When a straight current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to it, it experiences a force.

To find the direction of this force, we use Fleming’s left-hand rule.

Fleming’s left-hand rule:

Stretch the thumb, forefinger, and middle finger of your left hand so that they are mutually perpendicular.

Then:

  • Forefinger shows the direction of magnetic field

  • Middle finger shows the direction of current

  • Thumb shows the direction of force or motion

Why other options are wrong:

  • Right-hand thumb rule: Used to find the direction of magnetic field around a current-carrying conductor.

  • Fleming’s right-hand rule: Used for direction of induced current in electromagnetic induction.

  • Hund’s rule: Related to filling of electrons in atomic orbitals.

So, the answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Fleming’s left-hand rule}}$$

en.content: Which one of the following statements for second law of motion is NOT correct ?

options.content: The net force on a body is proportional to that body’s acceleration
The net force on a body is in the same direction as the acceleration
The time rate of change of momentum is equal to force
The net force on a body is proportional to that body’s momentum

en.explanation:
The statement which is NOT correct is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4: The net force on a body is proportional to that body’s momentum}}$$

Step-by-step:

According to Newton’s Second Law of Motion:

$$F = \frac{dp}{dt}$$

This means:

  • Force is equal to the time rate of change of momentum

  • If mass is constant, then:

$$F = ma$$

So:

  1. Option 1 is correct because force is proportional to acceleration.

  2. Option 2 is correct because acceleration is in the same direction as net force.

  3. Option 3 is correct because it is the actual statement of the second law.

  4. Option 4 is incorrect because force is not proportional to momentum, but to the rate of change of momentum.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4}}$$

en.content: Which one of the following statements for the work done by gravity on a body is NOT correct ?

options.content: Work done is independent of the path followed by the body
Work done depends only on the vertical distance separating the initial and the final position of the body
Work done depends on the path followed by the body
Work done is zero if the body is displaced horizontally to the force of gravity

en.explanation:
The incorrect statement is:

Option 3: Work done depends on the path followed by the body

Step-by-step explanation

Gravity is a conservative force. For a conservative force:

  • Work done does not depend on the path

  • It depends only on the initial and final positions

For gravity, work done is given by:

$$ W = mg(h_1 - h_2) $$

where:

  • $m$ = mass of the body

  • $g$ = acceleration due to gravity

  • $h_1$ = initial height

  • $h_2$ = final height

So:

  • If only the vertical height changes, gravity does work.

  • If the displacement is purely horizontal, then there is no change in height, so work done by gravity is zero.

Checking each option

  1. Work done is independent of the path followed by the body

    This is correct.

  2. Work done depends only on the vertical distance separating the initial and the final position of the body

    This is correct.

  3. Work done depends on the path followed by the body

    This is not correct.

  4. Work done is zero if the body is displaced horizontally to the force of gravity

    This is correct.

Final answer

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 3}} $$

en.explanation:
The correct statement is:

$$\text{Option 4: Pascal and } N/m^2 \text{ represent the same unit}$$

Now let us check each option one by one:

  1. Pressure has magnitude as well as direction

    This is wrong because pressure is a scalar quantity.

    It has only magnitude, not direction.

  2. As we go deeper in a liquid, the pressure decreases

    This is wrong.

    In a liquid, pressure increases with depth.

  3. Action and reaction forces act on the same object

    This is wrong.

    According to Newton’s third law, action and reaction act on different objects.

  4. Pascal and $N/m^2$ represent the same unit

    This is correct because

$$1 \, \text{pascal} = 1 \, N/m^2$$

So, the correct answer is Option 4.

  2. For better magnification in a telescope, the objective lens should have a large focal length.

  3. The eyepiece acts like a magnifying glass, so it has a small focal length.

Therefore, compared to the eyepiece, the objective lens has:

  • larger focal length

  • larger aperture

Hence, the answer is:

$$ [E] = ML^2T^{-2} $$

Step 2: Write dimensions of frequency

Frequency is the number of oscillations per second, so:

$$ [f] = T^{-1} $$

Step 3: Find dimensions of $h$

From

$$ h = \frac{E}{f} $$

So,

$$ [h] = \frac{ML^2T^{-2}}{T^{-1}} = ML^2T^{-1} $$

Step 4: Compare with given options

  • Linear momentum:

$$ p = mv $$

Dimensions:

$$ [p] = MLT^{-1} $$

Not same.

  • Angular momentum:

$$ L = mvr $$

Dimensions:

$$ [L] = MLT^{-1} \cdot L = ML^2T^{-1} $$

Same.

  • Displacement:

$$ [L] $$

Not same.

  • Torque:

$$ \tau = \text{force} \times \text{distance} $$

Dimensions:

$$ [\tau] = (MLT^{-2})(L) = ML^2T^{-2} $$

Not same.

Correct option:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 2: angular momentum}} $$

en.content: Why a fielder in the ground gradually pulls his hands backward while catching a fast moving cricket ball ?

options.content: To reduce the acceleration of the ball
To increase the acceleration of the ball
To increase the velocity of the ball

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 1: To reduce the acceleration of the ball.

Explanation:

When a fielder catches a fast-moving cricket ball, the ball has a large momentum. To stop it, the fielder has to bring its momentum to zero.

We know:

$$ \text{Force} = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} $$

where:

  • $\Delta p$ = change in momentum

  • $\Delta t$ = time taken

The change in momentum is fixed because the ball has to be stopped. So, if the fielder pulls his hands backward, the time of stopping $\Delta t$ increases.

When time increases, the force decreases.

Also, since acceleration is related to force by

$$ F = ma $$

less force means less acceleration (actually less retardation).

So, by moving his hands backward, the fielder reduces the acceleration of the ball, and therefore the force on his hands becomes less. This prevents injury.

Final Answer:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1: To reduce the acceleration of the ball}} $$

en.content: Suppose, a ball of mass M is thrown upwards from a point A and it reaches up to the highest point B and returns back to point A, which one among the following is correct ?

options.content: Kinetic Energy at A = Potential Energy at B
Kinetic Energy at A = Potential Energy at A
Kinetic Energy at B = Potential Energy at B
Kinetic Energy at B = Kinetic Energy at A

en.explanation:
The correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 1: Kinetic Energy at A = Potential Energy at B}}$$

Step-by-step explanation:

Let the ball be thrown upward from point $A$ with initial speed $u$.

At point $A$:

  • The ball has kinetic energy

$$KE_A = \frac{1}{2}Mu^2$$

  • If we take point $A$ as the reference level, then

$$PE_A = 0$$

At highest point $B$:

  • The velocity becomes zero, so

$$KE_B = 0$$

  • Let the height reached be $h$, then potential energy at $B$ is

$$PE_B = Mgh$$

Now, by conservation of mechanical energy:

$$\frac{1}{2}Mu^2 = Mgh$$

So,

$$KE_A = PE_B$$

Hence, Option 1 is correct.

Why other options are wrong:

  • Option 2: At point $A$, potential energy is usually taken as zero, so it is not equal to kinetic energy.

  • Option 3: At point $B$, kinetic energy is zero but potential energy is not zero.

  • Option 4: At point $B$, kinetic energy is zero, but at $A$ it is maximum.

$$\boxed{\text{Correct Answer: Option 1}}$$

en.explanation:

The reaction is:

$$ \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O} $$

Explanation:

  • Lime water is a solution of calcium hydroxide, that is, $\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}$.

  • When carbon dioxide is passed through lime water, it reacts to form:

  • calcium carbonate $\mathrm{(CaCO_3)}$, which is a white precipitate

  • water $\mathrm{(H_2O)}$

So lime water turns milky due to the formation of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$.

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O}} $$

So, Option 2 is correct.

$$\text{Option 2}$$

Reason:

Plaster of Paris has the formula:

$$\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

But in Option 2, it is given as:

$$\text{CaSO}_4 : 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This is wrong.

Checking the other options:

  1. Water of crystallization is the fixed number of water molecules present in one formula unit of a salt — correct.

  2. Plaster of Paris formula given is wrong — not correct.

  3. Washing soda is used for removing permanent hardness of water — correct.

  4. Venus has thick white and yellow clouds of sulphuric acid — correct.

So, the answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 2}}$$

en.content: Which one of the following is NOT a chemical change ?
en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 3: Melting of ice.

Reason:

A chemical change is a change in which a new substance is formed.

Let us check each option:

  1. Burning of coal

    Coal burns and forms new substances like carbon dioxide, ash, etc.

    So, it is a chemical change.

  2. Rusting of metal

    Iron reacts with oxygen and moisture to form rust.

    Rust is a new substance.

    So, it is a chemical change.

  3. Melting of ice

    Ice changes into water only. No new substance is formed.

    It is only a physical change.

    So, this is not a chemical change.

  4. Reaction of acid with base

    Acid and base react to form salt and water.

    New substances are formed.

    So, it is a chemical change.

Final Answer:

Option 3: Melting of ice

en.content: Which one of the following is NOT a heterogeneous mixture ?

options.content: Mixture of sugar 8 and salt crystals

en.explanation:
The correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 1: Sulphur in carbon disulphide}}$$

Step-by-step:

A heterogeneous mixture has non-uniform composition, and its different parts can be seen separately.

Now check each option:

  1. Sulphur in carbon disulphide

    Sulphur dissolves completely in carbon disulphide, so it forms a homogeneous mixture, not a heterogeneous mixture.

  2. Mixture of sugar and salt crystals

    Sugar crystals and salt crystals remain separate, so it is a heterogeneous mixture.

  3. Sandy water

    Sand does not dissolve in water, so it is a heterogeneous mixture.

  4. Sulphur in water

    Sulphur does not dissolve in water, so it is a heterogeneous mixture.

Therefore, the one which is not a heterogeneous mixture is:

$$\boxed{\text{Sulphur in carbon disulphide}}$$

en.content:
In the following redox reaction, which compound is acting as a reducing agent ?

N2H4 + 2H2O2 → N2 + 4H2O
en.explanation:
To find the reducing agent, we check which substance gets oxidised.

Given reaction:

$$ N_2H_4 + 2H_2O_2 \rightarrow N_2 + 4H_2O $$

Step 1: Find oxidation state of nitrogen in $N_2H_4$

Let oxidation state of nitrogen be $x$.

In $N_2H_4$:

  • Hydrogen has oxidation state $+1$

So,

$$ 2x + 4(+1) = 0 $$

$$ 2x + 4 = 0 $$

$$ 2x = -4 $$

$$ x = -2 $$

So, oxidation state of nitrogen in $N_2H_4$ is $-2$.

Step 2: Find oxidation state of nitrogen in $N_2$

In elemental form,

$$ \text{Oxidation state of } N \text{ in } N_2 = 0 $$

So nitrogen changes from:

$$ -2 \rightarrow 0 $$

This is oxidation.

Step 3: Identify reducing agent

The substance which gets oxidised acts as the reducing agent.

Here, $N_2H_4$ is oxidised to $N_2$.

Therefore, the reducing agent is:

$$ \boxed{N_2H_4} $$

Correct option: 2. $N_2H_4$

B. $CaCO_3(s) \to CaO(s) + CO_2(g)$

Here, one compound breaks down into simpler substances.

Here, the ions of two compounds exchange places.

So, this is a double displacement reaction.

$$D \to 2$$

$$A - 3,\; B - 4,\; C - 1,\; D - 2$$

Correct option: Option 3

en.content: Which one of the following is an endothermic reaction ?
en.explanation:
The endothermic reaction is:

Option 4

$$ 2Pb(NO_3)_2(s) \rightarrow 2PbO(s) + 4NO_2(g) + O_2(g) $$

Reason:

  • Endothermic reaction is a reaction in which heat is absorbed.

  • Thermal decomposition reactions usually need heat to break down a compound.

  • Lead nitrate, $Pb(NO_3)_2$, decomposes on heating, so this is an endothermic reaction.

Why others are not endothermic:

  • Option 1: Combustion of methane is exothermic.

  • Option 2: Respiration/glucose oxidation is exothermic.

  • Option 3: Formation of ammonia is also exothermic.

Correct answer:

Option 4

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 4: $CO + H_2$

Explanation:

Water gas is a mixture of:

$$ CO + H_2 $$

It is formed when steam passes over red hot carbon.

The reaction is:

$$ C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 $$

So, water gas contains carbon monoxide and hydrogen.

Correct option: Option 4

en.content: Which one of the following statements is NOT correct ?

options.content: Diamond is hard due to extended covalent bonding in it
Fullerene is composed of five and six membered rings
Diamond is a good conductor of electricity
Graphite is used as a lubricant

en.explanation:
The incorrect statement is:

Option 3: Diamond is a good conductor of electricity

Step-by-step explanation:

1. Diamond

In diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms by strong covalent bonds, forming a rigid three-dimensional network.

So:

  • Diamond is very hard

  • This is due to extended covalent bonding

  • But diamond does not conduct electricity, because it has no free electrons

So Option 1 is correct.

2. Fullerene

Fullerene is a form of carbon made up of five-membered and six-membered rings.

So Option 2 is correct.

3. Graphite

Graphite has layers of carbon atoms. These layers can slide over one another easily.

So:

  • Graphite is used as a lubricant

  • It also conducts electricity due to the presence of free electrons

So Option 4 is correct.

Final Answer:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 3}}$$

en.content: The optimum pH at which the enzymes pepsin and trypsin of the human alimentary canal works are
en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 1: pH $2.0$ and $7.9$, respectively.

Step-by-step:

  1. Pepsin works in the stomach.

  • The stomach has a highly acidic medium due to hydrochloric acid.

  • So, pepsin works best at about pH $2.0$.

  1. Trypsin works in the small intestine.

  • The small intestine has an alkaline medium.

  • So, trypsin works best at about pH $7.9$.

Therefore,

$$ \text{Pepsin} \rightarrow pH\,2.0,\qquad \text{Trypsin} \rightarrow pH\,7.9 $$

So, the answer is Option 1.

en.content: The amount of air breathed in and out during a normal respiration by human lungs is referred to as
en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 2: Tidal volume.

Here is a step-by-step explanation of the terms based on the NCERT textbook to help you understand why this is the correct answer:

1. Tidal Volume (TV)

According to your textbook, Tidal Volume is defined as the volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration. For a healthy man, this volume is approximately $500 \text{ mL}$ per inspiration or expiration. Therefore, this matches the description given in your question perfectly.

Let us also look at the other options so you can understand what they mean:

2. Vital Capacity (VC)

This is the maximum volume of air a person can breathe in after a forced expiration. It includes the Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV), Tidal Volume (TV), and Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV). It is not the volume during normal respiration.

3. Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)

This refers to the additional volume of air a person can inspire by a forcible inspiration, over and above the normal Tidal Volume. It averages $2500 \text{ mL}$ to $3000 \text{ mL}$.

4. Total Lung Capacity (TLC)

This is the total volume of air accommodated in the lungs at the end of a forced inspiration. It includes Residual Volume (RV), Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV), Tidal Volume (TV), and Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV).

Since the question specifically asks for the amount of air breathed in and out during normal respiration, Tidal volume is the correct biological term.

hi.content: मानव फेफड़ों (फुप्फुस ) द्वारा सामान्य श्वसन के दौरान प्रति श्वास बायु को अंदर खींचने और वायु को बाहर मुक्त करने की मात्रा को किस रूप में व्यक्त किया जाता है ?
hi.explanation:
सही उत्तर है विकल्प 2: आवृत्ति आयतन (Tidal volume)।

यहाँ आपके एनसीईआरटी पाठ्यपुस्तक के आधार पर इन शब्दों की चरण-दर-चरण व्याख्या प्रस्तुत है, जिससे आपको समझने में मदद मिलेगी कि यह उत्तर क्यों सही है:

1. आवृत्ति आयतन (Tidal Volume - TV)

आपकी पाठ्यपुस्तक के अनुसार, आवृत्ति आयतन वह वायु आयतन है, जो सामान्य श्वसन के दौरान अंदर लिया या बाहर निकाला जाता है। एक स्वस्थ पुरुष में यह मात्रा लगभग $500 \text{ mL}$ प्रति श्वसन होती है। अतः यह आपके प्रश्न में दी गई परिभाषा से पूरी तरह मेल खाती है।

आइए अन्य विकल्पों को भी देखें ताकि आप समझ सकें कि वे क्या अर्थ रखते हैं:

2. जीवन क्षमता (Vital Capacity - VC)

यह वायु की अधिकतम मात्रा है, जिसे कोई व्यक्ति जबर्दस्ती श्वसन के बाद अंदर ले सकता है। इसमें निष्कासनीय संचित आयतन (Expiratory Reserve Volume - ERV), आवृत्ति आयतन (TV) एवं प्रेरणीय संचित आयतन (Inspiratory Reserve Volume - IRV) सम्मिलित होते हैं। यह सामान्य श्वसन के दौरान वायु की मात्रा नहीं है।

3. प्रेरणीय संचित आयतन (Inspiratory Reserve Volume - IRV)

यह अतिरिक्त वायु आयतन है, जिसे कोई व्यक्ति सामान्य आवृत्ति आयतन के अतिरिक्त, जबर्दस्ती श्वसन द्वारा अंदर ले सकता है। इसका औसत $2500 \text{ mL}$ से $3000 \text{ mL}$ होता है।

4. कुल फुफ्फुसीय क्षमता (Total Lung Capacity - TLC)

यह फेफड़ों में जबरदस्ती श्वसन के अंत में समाहित वायु का कुल आयतन है। इसमें अवशिष्ट आयतन (Residual Volume - RV), निष्कासनीय संचित आयतन (ERV), आवृत्ति आयतन (TV) एवं प्रेरणीय संचित आयतन (IRV) सम्मिलित होते हैं।

चूँकि प्रश्न में सामान्य श्वसन के दौरान अंदर ली या बाहर निकाली गई वायु की मात्रा पूछी गई है, इसलिए जैविक रूप से सही शब्द "आवृत्ति आयतन" (Tidal Volume) है।

en.content: Which one among the following statements about arteries and veins in humans is NOT correct ?

options.content: Arteries carry blood away from heart while veins bring blood towards the heart
Pulmonary arteries always carry oxygenated blood while Pulmonary veins always carry deoxygenated blood
Arteries have thicker and more elastic walls compared to veins in general
Arteries do not have valves but veins do have valves

en.explanation:
Pulmonary arteries always carry oxygenated blood while pulmonary veins always carry deoxygenated blood is NOT correct.

Explanation:

Role of Arteries and Veins:

Arteries and veins move blood in our body.

Usually, arteries carry oxygen-rich (oxygenated) blood from the heart to the body. Veins bring back oxygen-poor (deoxygenated) blood from the body to the heart.

Exception in Pulmonary Circulation:

In the case of the lungs, this rule is opposite:

The pulmonary artery takes deoxygenated blood from the heart (right ventricle) to the lungs, so it can get oxygen.

The pulmonary vein brings oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the heart (left atrium).

So, here arteries carry deoxygenated blood and veins carry oxygenated blood, which is the opposite of the usual rule.

hi.content: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->मानव में धमनियों और शिराओं के बारे में निम्नलिखित में से कौन-सा कथन सही नहीं है ?

options.content: धमनियां हृदय से रक्त ले जाती हैं जबकि शिराएं हृदय की ओर रक्त लाती है
फुप्फुसी धमनियां सदैव ऑक्सीजनयुक्त रक्त ले जाती हैं जबकि फुफ्फुसी शिराएं सदैव ऑक्सीजनरहित रक्त ले जाती हैं।
सामान्यतः शिराओं की तुलना में धमनियों की दीवारें अधिक मोटी और लचीली होती हैं।
धमनियों में वाल्व नहीं होते हैं किंतु शिराओं में वाल्व होते हैं

hi.explanation:
"फुफ्फुसीय धमनी (Pulmonary arteries) हमेशा ऑक्सीजन युक्त रक्त ले जाती हैं जबकि फुफ्फुसीय शिरा (Pulmonary veins) हमेशा ऑक्सीजन रहित रक्त ले जाती हैं" कथन सही नहीं है।

व्याख्या:

धमनी और शिरा की भूमिका:

धमनी और शिरा हमारे शरीर में रक्त परिसंचरण का कार्य करती हैं।

सामान्यतः, धमनी (artery) हृदय से शरीर के विभिन्न भागों तक ऑक्सीजन युक्त (ऑक्सीजनित) रक्त पहुंचाती हैं। शिराएं (vein) शरीर के विभिन्न भागों से ऑक्सीजन रहित (डिऑक्सीजनेटेड) रक्त हृदय की ओर लौटाती हैं।

फुफ्फुसीय परिसंचरण में अपवाद:

फेफड़ों के मामले में यह नियम उल्टा होता है:

फुफ्फुसीय धमनी (Pulmonary artery) हृदय के दाहिने निलय (right ventricle) से ऑक्सीजन रहित रक्त को फेफड़ों तक ले जाती है, जिससे रक्त ऑक्सीजन प्राप्त कर सके।

फुफ्फुसीय शिरा (Pulmonary vein) फेफड़ों से ऑक्सीजन युक्त रक्त हृदय के बाएं आलिंद (left atrium) में वापस लाती है।

इस प्रकार, यहाँ धमनी डिऑक्सीजनेटेड रक्त ले जाती है और शिरा ऑक्सीजनित रक्त लाती है, जो सामान्य नियम के विपरीत है।

en.content: Identify the functions that are controlled by the hind brain of human being

options.content: Involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation vomiting ete.
Controlling the regions receiving sensory impulses from receptors
Controlling the movement of voluntary muscles
Sensation of hunger or being full

en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 1: Involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation vomiting etc.

Let us understand this step-by-step by looking at the divisions of the human brain as described in your NCERT textbook:

  1. The human brain is divided into three major parts or regions: the fore-brain, mid-brain, and hind-brain.

  2. The hind-brain plays a major role in controlling involuntary actions (actions that happen without our conscious thought). It consists of three parts: the pons, medulla, and cerebellum.

According to the NCERT textbook, involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation, and vomiting are strictly controlled by the medulla present in the hind-brain. Therefore, Option 1 is correct.

Let us also look at the other options to understand why they are incorrect:

  • Option 2 (Sensory impulses): The fore-brain is the main thinking part of the brain. It possesses specialized regions that receive sensory impulses from various receptors for hearing, smell, sight, etc.

  • Option 3 (Voluntary muscles): The fore-brain contains motor areas that control the movement of voluntary muscles (like your leg muscles when you decide to walk). While a part of the hind-brain (the cerebellum) helps in the precision and balance of these movements, the actual control and decision of voluntary movement come from the fore-brain.

  • Option 4 (Sensation of hunger): The sensation of feeling hungry or being full is controlled by a separate, specific centre associated with the fore-brain.

Thus, comparing all the functions, Option 1 is the correct function controlled by the hind-brain.

hi.content: उन प्रकार्यों की पहचान कीजिए जो मनुष्य के पश्च मस्तिष्क द्वारा नियंत्रित किए जाते हैं ?

options.content: अनैच्छिक क्रियाएं जैसे रक्तदाब, लार आना, वमन आदि
ग्राही अंगों से संवेदी आवेग प्राप्त करने वाले क्षेत्रों का नियंत्रण
स्वैच्छिक मांसपेशियों की गति को नियंत्रित करना
भूख या पेट भरा होने की संवेदना

hi.explanation:
सही उत्तर है विकल्प 1: अनैच्छिक क्रियाएँ जैसे रक्तचाप, लार आना, उल्टी आदि।

आइए इसे चरण-दर-चरण समझते हैं और आपकी एनसीईआरटी पाठ्यपुस्तक में वर्णित मानव मस्तिष्क के विभाजनों को देखते हैं:

  1. मानव मस्तिष्क को तीन प्रमुख भागों या क्षेत्रों में बाँटा गया है: अग्र मस्तिष्क (fore-brain), मध्य मस्तिष्क (mid-brain), और पश्च मस्तिष्क (hind-brain)।

  2. पश्च मस्तिष्क (hind-brain) अनैच्छिक क्रियाओं (वे क्रियाएँ जो हमारी चेतना के बिना होती हैं) को नियंत्रित करने में मुख्य भूमिका निभाता है। इसमें तीन भाग होते हैं: पोंस, मेडुला और सेरिबेलम।

एनसीईआरटी पाठ्यपुस्तक के अनुसार, रक्तचाप, लार आना और उल्टी जैसी अनैच्छिक क्रियाओं का नियंत्रण मुख्य रूप से पश्च मस्तिष्क में उपस्थित मेडुला द्वारा किया जाता है। अतः विकल्प 1 सही है।

आइए अन्य विकल्पों को भी देखें कि वे क्यों गलत हैं:

  • विकल्प 2 (संवेदी आवेग): अग्र मस्तिष्क (fore-brain) मस्तिष्क का मुख्य सोचने वाला भाग है। इसमें विशेष क्षेत्र होते हैं, जो श्रवण, गंध, दृश्य आदि के विभिन्न अभिग्राहकों से संवेदी आवेग प्राप्त करते हैं।

  • विकल्प 3 (इच्छायुक्त पेशियाँ): अग्र मस्तिष्क (fore-brain) में वह क्षेत्र होता है जो इच्छायुक्त पेशियों (जैसे पैरों की मांसपेशियाँ जब आप चलने का निर्णय लेते हैं) की गति नियंत्रित करता है। यद्यपि पश्च मस्तिष्क का एक भाग (सेरिबेलम) इन गतियों की सटीकता और संतुलन में सहायता करता है, लेकिन असल नियंत्रण और इच्छित गति का निर्णय अग्र मस्तिष्क से आता है।

  • विकल्प 4 (भूख का अहसास): भूख लगना या तृप्ति का अनुभव होना अग्र मस्तिष्क से जुड़े विशेष केंद्र द्वारा नियंत्रित किया जाता है।

इस प्रकार, सभी कार्यों की तुलना करने पर, विकल्प 1 वह सही कार्य है जिसे पश्च मस्तिष्क नियंत्रित करता है।

en.content: Iodized salt is a vital part of our diet as it is essential for the synthesis of
en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 2: Thyroxin

Explanation:

  1. Iodine is a micronutrient required by our body in very small amounts.

  2. The thyroid gland, located in the neck, uses iodine to produce the hormone thyroxin.

  3. Thyroxin controls the metabolic rate of the body and helps in growth and development.

  4. A deficiency of iodine leads to goitre, a disease in which the thyroid gland enlarges.

Therefore, iodized salt is included in our diet to provide iodine for the synthesis of thyroxin.

en.content: The menstrual cycle in females is controlled by hormones that are secreted by
en.explanation:
The correct option is:

Option 3: Ovary and pituitary gland

Explanation:

  1. The pituitary gland (present in the brain) secretes two important hormones:

  • Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH)

  • Luteinising Hormone (LH)

    These hormones act on the ovaries.

  1. The ovaries respond to these hormones and secrete:

  • Oestrogen

  • Progesterone

  1. Together, these hormones regulate different stages of the menstrual cycle — the maturation of the egg, release of the egg (ovulation), and preparation of the uterus for possible pregnancy.

Hence, the menstrual cycle is controlled by the ovary and pituitary gland.

en.content: Which one of the following blood cells type secrete large number of antibodies during am immune response of the body?
en.explanation:
The correct answer is:

Option 3: Lymphocytes

Explanation:

During an immune response, B-lymphocytes get activated and change into plasma cells. These plasma cells secrete a large number of antibodies.

Why not the others?

  • Neutrophils: They mainly engulf and destroy microbes.

  • Eosinophils: They are involved in allergic reactions and defense against parasites.

  • Monocytes: They become macrophages and help in phagocytosis.

So, the blood cells that secrete large numbers of antibodies are lymphocytes.

en.content: Which of the following organelles have their own DNA and ribosomes ?
en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option 2: Mitochondria and Chloroplast.

Explanation:

Mitochondria and chloroplast are called semi-autonomous organelles because they have:

  • their own DNA

  • their own ribosomes

This helps them to make some of their own proteins.

Why not the other options?

  • Endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi bodies do not have their own DNA or ribosomes.

  • Cytoskeleton does not have DNA or ribosomes.

  • Cell wall is a non-living covering, and nucleolus does not have its own DNA and ribosomes like mitochondria and chloroplast.

Thus, the answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Mitochondria and Chloroplast}}$$

en.content:
Consider the following statements with regard to National Technology Day :

1. It commemorates the momentous events of 11th May 1998, when India conducted successful nuclear tests under Operation Shakti

2. The official theme for National Technology Day 2025 is ‘School  to Startups — Igniting Young Minds to Innovate’

Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
en.explanation:
Statement 1

"It commemorates the momentous events of 11th May 1998, when India conducted successful nuclear tests under Operation Shakti."

  • National Technology Day is observed every year on 11th May in India.

  • On this day in 1998, India successfully carried out nuclear tests at Pokhran, Rajasthan, known as Pokhran-II or Operation Shakti, under the leadership of then Prime Minister Shri Atal Bihari Vajpayee.

  • On the same day, India also successfully test-fired the Hansa-3 aircraft and the Trishul missile.

  • Recognising these achievements, Shri Atal Bihari Vajpayee declared 11th May as National Technology Day.

Hence, Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2

"The official theme for National Technology Day 2025 is 'School to Startups — Igniting Young Minds to Innovate.'"

  • The theme "From Schools to Startups: Igniting Young Minds to Innovate" was actually the theme for National Technology Day 2024, not 2025.

  • Therefore, the theme mentioned here has been wrongly attributed to the year 2025.

Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.

Correct Answer

Option 1: 1 only

Quick Recap

  • Date: 11th May (every year)

  • First observed: 1999

  • Commemorates: Pokhran-II nuclear tests (Operation Shakti), Hansa-3 test flight, and Trishul missile test — all in 1998.

  • Nodal Agency: Technology Development Board (TDB), under the Department of Science and Technology.
